1.synthesis: YHWH's answer to the drought liturgy's prayers in chapter 14 is devastating in chapter 15: even the most powerful intercessors in Israel's history cannot change the verdict. Though Moses and Samuel stood before me, yet my heart would not turn toward this people. Send them out of my sight, and let them go (Jeremiah 15:1). Moses had interceded successfully at the golden calf (Exodus 32:11–14) and at Taberah; Samuel had interceded at Mizpah (1 Samuel 7:5–9). These are the two supreme examples of prophetic intercession in Israel's memory. If they cannot turn YHWH's heart, no intercession can. The fourfold destiny of Jeremiah 15:2—those for pestilence, to pestilence; those for sword, to sword; those for famine, to famine; those for captivity, to captivity—is a comprehensive distribution of covenant-curse categories across the population. No one escapes; each receives their appointed share of judgment. The Manasseh-attribution of Jeremiah 15:4—I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth on account of what Manasseh the son of Hezekiah, king of Judah, did in Jerusalem—gives the judgment historical grounding: Manasseh's decades of apostasy (including child sacrifice at Hinnom) accumulated a covenant debt that Josiah's reform could not fully cancel. The lament of Jeremiah 15:5–9 is voiced by YHWH himself: who will have pity on you, O Jerusalem, or who will grieve for you? The compassion language of pity and grieve reveals YHWH's own grief even as he enacts judgment. The mother-of-seven-grown-faint image (Jeremiah 15:9) captures the depletion of Judah's most blessed matron: she bore seven sons, the number of completeness, yet even she faints when her sons are taken. The sun setting while it is still day is the utter disruption of the created order that accompanies covenant judgment.
1.historical_context: Manasseh's reign (698–643 BCE) was the longest of any Judahite king and the most religiously catastrophic: he reversed Hezekiah's reforms, erected altars to Baal in the Temple courts, practiced child sacrifice in the Valley of Hinnom, and persecuted prophetic voices (2 Kings 21:1–18). The Deuteronomistic Historian attributes the Babylonian exile specifically to Manasseh's sins (2 Kings 23:26–27). YHWH's statement that even Moses and Samuel cannot intercede points to the irreversibility that Manasseh's cumulative apostasy has produced.
1.literary_structure: The unit moves through the intercession-impossibility (v. 1), the fourfold destiny distribution (v. 2), the historical attribution to Manasseh (vv. 3–4), and YHWH's own lament over the consequences (vv. 5–9). The movement from legal verdict to personal grief mirrors the dual character of divine justice in the prophets: judgment is both judicially necessary and emotionally costly for YHWH himself.
1.theological_application: The Moses-and-Samuel statement raises the theological question of the limits of intercession: not because YHWH is capricious but because covenant consequences accumulate to a point where justice requires their fulfillment. Even the most powerful human advocates cannot substitute for genuine covenant repentance. The Manasseh attribution models intergenerational accountability: the sins of rulers affect communities across multiple generations, and structural evil has long covenant half-lives that individual reforms cannot always fully cancel.
1.christology: The inadequacy of even Moses' and Samuel's intercession (Jeremiah 15:1) points to the need for an intercessor whose advocacy is grounded in something more than prophetic standing. Hebrews identifies Christ as the mediator of a better covenant precisely because his intercession is grounded in his own atoning sacrifice, not merely his moral authority (Hebrews 7:25–27). Where Moses and Samuel could only appeal to covenant history, Christ appeals to his own blood.

10.synthesis: The fifth Confession begins with Jeremiah's famous birth-curse: Woe is me, my mother, that you bore me, a man of strife and contention to the whole land! I have not lent, nor have I borrowed, yet all of them curse me (Jeremiah 15:10). The birth-curse is the prophet's declaration that his existence has brought him nothing but conflict. In the ANE, lending and borrowing were primary sources of social conflict; Jeremiah's protest is that he has not even engaged in those normal economic activities that typically generate enemies, yet the whole land curses him. His only provocation was his prophetic message. YHWH's response in Jeremiah 15:11—Have I not set you free for their good? Have I not pleaded for you before the enemy in the time of trouble and in the time of distress?—is a reminder of divine protection already given and the wider purpose behind the prophet's suffering: he speaks for the people's good even when they hate him for it. The metallurgical images of Jeremiah 15:12—Can iron break iron from the north, and bronze?—suggest that the coming Babylonian power cannot be resisted by ordinary means. YHWH then shifts to speak directly of Judah's judgment: Your wealth and your treasures I will give as spoil, without price, for all your sins, throughout all your territory (Jeremiah 15:13). The without price (lo bikhmah) echoes the chinnam motif of Isaiah 52: Israel is handed over for nothing, just as she was once sold for nothing in Egypt. Exile to an unknown land (Jeremiah 15:14) closes the unit: YHWH's anger is a kindled fire that does not go out until it has consumed what it was sent to consume.
10.historical_context: Jeremiah's birth-curse (v. 10) is the first of the explicit self-cursing expressions that culminate in ch. 20:14–18. The social isolation of the prophet — cursed by his community despite his faithful service — was genuine and documented throughout the book. The iron-from-the-north imagery (v. 12) is consistent with Jeremiah's recurring use of the “foe from the north” motif to describe Babylon, a power whose military technology and organizational strength were superior to anything Judah could field.
10.literary_structure: The section moves through Jeremiah's birth-curse complaint (v. 10), YHWH's reminder of past protection (v. 11), the metallurgical impossibility (v. 12), and the judgment decree against Judah (vv. 13–14). The prophet's personal complaint (v. 10) is interrupted by YHWH who addresses not only the prophet's suffering but Judah's coming fate, revealing that the Confession genre in Jeremiah is never purely personal but always embedded in the covenant crisis the prophet is navigating.
10.theological_application: Jeremiah's protest (I have not lent or borrowed, yet all curse me) articulates the anguish of serving a community that resents its intercessor. YHWH's response does not deny the suffering but situates it within a larger purpose: Jeremiah's speech is for their good, even when they hate him for it. This is the pastoral vocation at its most costly: speaking covenant truth to a community that experiences it as condemnation, and continuing to do so because the alternative is abandonment to their own destruction.
10.christology: Jeremiah's birth-curse anticipates the pattern of the rejected prophet that Jesus identifies in Matthew 23:37: Jerusalem stones the prophets and kills those sent to her. Christ himself bore the curse of communal rejection for the sake of those who rejected him (Galatians 3:13). The fire-kindled anger that does not go out (v. 14) is taken up in the New Testament's language of unquenchable judgment (Matthew 3:12), while Christ himself is presented as the one who exhausts that fire.

15.synthesis: The fullest of Jeremiah's Confessions continues with a deeply personal petition and one of the most intimate descriptions of prophetic calling in the Bible. Jeremiah's prayer of Jeremiah 15:15–18 moves through petition (O YHWH, you know; remember me and visit me, and take vengeance for me on my persecutors), testimony (your words were found, and I ate them, and your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart), isolation (I did not sit in the company of merrymakers, nor did I rejoice; I sat alone, because your hand was upon me), and then the most daring accusation: Why is my pain unceasing, my wound incurable, refusing to be healed? Will you be to me like a deceitful brook, like waters that fail? (Jeremiah 15:18). The deceitful brook (nahal akhzav) is a wadi that fills with winter rain and appears reliable but runs completely dry in summer when it is needed most. Jeremiah accuses YHWH of being like a water source that fails at the critical moment—the very opposite of the fountain of living waters he had called YHWH in Jeremiah 2:13. YHWH's response is a conditional recommissioning (Jeremiah 15:19): If you return, I will restore you, and you shall stand before me. If you utter what is precious, and not what is worthless, you shall be as my mouth. The prophet's continued calling depends on his returning (shuv) from spiritual crisis back to covenant fidelity. They shall turn to him, not he to them. YHWH then renews the protective commission: I will make you to this people a fortified wall of bronze; they will fight against you, but they shall not prevail over you (Jeremiah 15:20). The bronze-wall image echoes the original call of Jeremiah 1:18, restated after the prophet's deepest crisis. The closing promise of rescue—I will deliver you out of the hand of the wicked and redeem you from the grasp of the ruthless (Jeremiah 15:21)—uses the vocabulary of the Exodus redemption for Jeremiah's personal rescue.
15.historical_context: The word-eating image of Jeremiah 15:16—your words were found, and I ate them—parallels Ezekiel's scroll-eating vision (Ezekiel 3:1–3) and the Revelation of John (Revelation 10:9–10), all describing the internalization of the divine word as a bodily, digestive act. The deceitful-brook accusation (v. 18) is the most theologically confronting complaint in the Confessions: it accuses YHWH of the very deception that Jeremiah had suffered at the hands of false prophets. YHWH's response does not rebuke the accusation but calls the prophet back to his covenant posture.
15.literary_structure: The Confession moves through petition (v. 15), testimony of word-joy (v. 16), isolation-description (v. 17), and the deceitful-brook accusation (v. 18), then YHWH's conditional recommissioning (v. 19) and bronze-wall promise (vv. 20–21). The structure is a complete lament cycle: complaint → petition → accusation → divine response → renewed commission. The return/restore wordplay (shuv / shavtikha) in v. 19 is a key Hebrew pun.
15.theological_application: The deceitful-brook accusation models the permissibility of honest spiritual crisis before God. Jeremiah does not suppress his experience of divine abandonment but articulates it with maximum force. YHWH's response is not rebuke but recommissioning with a condition: the prophet must return from his crisis to his covenant posture before he can continue as YHWH's mouth. This models that spiritual renewal requires honest self-examination and re-orientation rather than simple reassurance. The word-eating joy of v. 16 is the prophetic resource that sustains through isolation.
15.christology: The word-eating testimony of Jeremiah 15:16 anticipates the Johannine theology of the word as bread: Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God (Matthew 4:4). Jesus himself is the Word who gives himself to be consumed as bread of life (John 6:35). The bronze-wall protection promised in v. 20 is echoed in Paul's declaration that no one who hopes in him will be put to shame (Romans 10:11). YHWH who is not a deceitful brook is fully revealed in Christ as the living water that never runs dry (John 4:14).
